General first data pull

Computations for depth x SWE

15-year graph with trend

trends over the years (for poster) with the M-P fitted line

Figure 1. Seasonal evolution of bulk snowpack density at the Deadman Hill SNOTEL site for water years 2005–2025, restricted to December 1 through April 30. Water years 2011 and 2012 are highlighted for comparison, while all remaining years are shown in gray. The red line shows a piecewise empirical density model using 𝜌=164.7+0.4” ” 𝑡_𝑊𝑌from December 1 to March 1 and 𝜌=−90.7+2.1” ” 𝑡_𝑊𝑌from March 1 to April 30 (1).

Daily SWE, depth, and density

Daily changes

2) Plot daily changes directly to see the pattern better

density decreases while depth increases, then later depth settles back down.

How to read that plot

  • Large positive depth bars with a drop in density suggest new, low-density snowfall
  • After that, depth may decline gradually while density rises, which suggests settling/compaction
  • The reverse pattern is usually less consistent with fresh snowfall

3) Classify likely snowfall vs settling events

compare each day’s density to a fresh snow reference density.

5) Scatterplot to distinguish snowfall vs settling

  • x = change in depth
  • y = change in density

That gives: - upper left / lower right type structure depending on process - fresh snowfall should often be depth up, density down - settling should often be depth down, density up

6) Add precipitation to confirm snowfall days

  • Precip > 0
  • SWE increases
  • Depth increases or stays similar
  • Density decreases or stays near fresh snow density

That is strong evidence for new snowfall

7) A cleaner event-based filter

This isolates the pattern:

when density spikes down, depth spikes up, then comes down later at a different settling rate

Step-by-step: modeled density → modeled depth → daily changes → difference plot
